top_categories.workout_context: At mile 60 of 143, glycogen is gone. This workout teaches your body to produce watts on fumes.
Race-pace efforts that mimic Dragon Ride L'Etape Wales' demands. Pacing, fueling, and tactical decisions under fatigue. Practice the race before June.
80ft/mi means long efforts at or near FTP on every climb. If your threshold cracks at minute 15, you'll walk the rest.
11,483ft of climbing means repeated surges above threshold. VO2max work is how you survive the fifth climb, not just the first.
Dragon Ride L'Etape Wales' paved Welsh mountain roads through the Brecon forces constant power changes. Surge over the rough stuff, settle on the smooth, repeat for 143 miles.
88–92% FTP is where you'll spend the hardest sustained miles of Dragon Ride L'Etape Wales. Train it until it feels boring.
Endurance with intensity spikes baked in. Exactly what 11,483ft of climbing on paved Welsh mountain roads through the Brecon does to your power file on race day.
At Dragon Ride L'Etape Wales, surges push you above threshold and you have to recover while still riding. Over-unders train exactly that.
143 miles rewards fat oxidation. This volume work shifts your fuel mix so you're still burning fat at hour 5, not bonking at hour 3.
Every hard session works better with a bigger aerobic base. At 143 miles, base fitness is the difference between racing and surviving.
Two threshold sessions per week without the recovery cost of VO2max work. For 143 miles, sustainable FTP gains matter more than peak power.
Tempo is Dragon Ride L'Etape Wales' race pace. The wattage you'll hold for 143 miles on paved Welsh mountain roads through the Brecon. Train it until it's automatic.

race_overlay.nutrition: Target 60–80g carbs/hour for Dragon Ride L'Etape Wales’s 143 miles. Start fueling within the first 30 minutes — early fueling prevents late-race collapse. Bonking at mile 60 is a nutrition failure, not a fitness failure.

pack_summary: This 10-workout pack focuses on Durability, Race Simulation, and TT Threshold to prepare you for 143 miles of paved welsh mountain roads through the brecon beacons national park in Margam Park, Port Talbot, South Wales, UK.
